query: differential diagnosis of acute dyspnea in young adults without risk factors for pulmonary embolism

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism -- History and Physical -- Clinical History
retrieved_docs.content: A timely diagnosis of PE is crucial because of the high associated mortality and morbidity, which may be prevented with early treatment. Notably, 30% of untreated patients with PEs die, while only 8% die after timely therapy. [22] [23] Unfortunately, diagnosing PE can be difficult due to the variety of nonspecific clinical signs and symptoms in patients with acute PE. The most common symptoms of PE include dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, cough, hemoptysis, presyncope, and syncope. Dyspnea may be acute and severe in a central PE, whereas mild and transient dyspnea is observed in small peripheral PE. In patients with preexisting heart failure or pulmonary disease, worsening dyspnea may be the only symptom. Chest pain is a frequent symptom usually caused by pleural irritation due to distal emboli causing pulmonary infarction. [24] In central PE, chest pain may be from underlying right ventricular ischemia and needs to be differentiated from an acute coronary syndrome or aortic dissection.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Infarction(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Given pulmonary embolism serves as the etiology of pulmonary infarction in most cases, it is important to understand the clinical picture representing both conditions. The presenting features of a pulmonary infarction overlap those of a pulmonary embolism with a few important distinctions. In patients with concurrent pulmonary embolism and pulmonary infarction, the following features are present: dyspnea (69% to 78%), chest pain (49% to 70%), swelling or pain in a unilateral lower extremity (27% to 31%), fever (5% to 11%), and hemoptysis (4% to 19%). [1] [3] [12] Presenting features in all patients with the diagnosis of pulmonary embolism, regardless of the presence of pulmonary infarction, included dyspnea (72% to 75%), chest pain (36% to 38%), signs of DVT (22% to 33%), hemoptysis (4% to 8%). [1] [20] Thus, pleuritic chest pain and hemoptysis were both more common in patients with pulmonary embolism and pulmonary infarction versus those with pulmonary embolism and no pulmonary infarction. Symptoms noted to have no statistical difference between the 2 groups include cough, syncope, sudden onset dyspnea, signs of DVT, fever, and right ventricular overload. Among patients incidentally found to have a pulmonary infarction after a biopsy of a radiographically discovered lung nodule, 65% of patients had no respiratory complaints, 26% had dyspnea, 7% had chest pain, and 5% had hemoptysis. [4] This data highlights the difficulty of diagnosing a pulmonary infarction and emphasizes the importance of considering a broad differential diagnosis for patients who present with symptoms that could be secondary to a pulmonary infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Acute pulmonary embolism (PE) occurs when a blood clot that has arisen from another area obstructs the pulmonary arteries. PE typically originates as a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in the lower extremities. Together, PE and DVT form the spectrum of venous thromboembolism (VTE). Symptoms of PE vary and are often nonspecific, including dyspnea, chest pain, cough, and syncope. Severe cases may involve hemodynamic instability and signs of right ventricular strain. Diagnosis combines clinical probability scoring systems like the Wells and Geneva criteria with tests such as D-dimer levels, CT pulmonary angiography, and ultrasound. Hemodynamically unstable patients may require bedside imaging for rapid diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Despite advancements in diagnostic tools and treatment options, the nonspecific symptoms of PE (eg, dyspnea, chest pain, and syncope) often overlap with other cardiovascular and respiratory conditions, making timely diagnosis challenging for clinicians. Prompt recognition and management of PE are critical, as delayed intervention can lead to severe complications, including hemodynamic instability, right ventricular failure, and sudden death. Risk stratification tools such as the Wells criteria and Geneva score, alongside diagnostic imaging, are essential for accurate diagnosis. However, the underutilization of these tools, coupled with variations in therapeutic approaches, underscores the need for standardized clinical protocols. Clinicians can improve patient outcomes through enhanced knowledge and proficiency in identifying risk factors, employing diagnostic strategies, and initiating evidence-based treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: Thromboembolic Event -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: With pulmonary embolism (PE), the most common presenting complaint is dyspnea that is sudden in onset. Patients may also demonstrate pleuritic chest pain and cough, as well as hemoptysis in some cases. Massive pulmonary embolism presents with syncope and signs of hemodynamic compromise such as hypotension and shock. Physical examination of patients with PE may reveal hypoxia, tachycardia, and fever. Tachypnea, with a respiratory rate over 18/min, is the most common sign of PE. However, in general, physical exam findings for PE are variable and nondiagnostic. The presence of confounding factors such as sickle cell disease may complicate presentation even further. In these patients, PE usually presents with a chest infection. In older patients, new-onset atrial fibrillation may be the presenting symptom of a PE and requires exclusion in such cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Dyspnea on Exertion(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial blood gas testing is used for this purpose as well as to calculate the A-a gradient and assess for an acidotic state. If PaO 2 is low with a normal chest x-ray, then pulmonary embolism (PE) should be considered. The pH is mostly alkalotic in the setting of PE. This is to blow carbon dioxide to relatively increase the partial pressure of oxygen. In a pregnant female, a d-dimer with leg ultrasound and V/Q scan should be ordered first. Detection of a mismatch in two or more areas indicates pulmonary embolism. D-dimer testing has low specificity and high sensitivity. Spiral CT of the chest is an alternative to V/Q scanning. In acute settings, the CT chest with PE protocol is the gold standard. If the dyspnea on exertion is chronic, then chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) should be considered, and the VQ scan is the test of choice and is considered the gold standard. The VQ scan in this setting has a “moth-eaten” appearance.

retrieved_docs.title: Costochondritis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Intrathoracic Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary embolism: Consider this disorder in patients with chest pain and dyspnea. Risk factors include a history of malignancy, recent travel, recent surgery or injury, personal history of pulmonary embolism or deep vein thrombosis, tobacco use, and oral contraceptive intake. The Wells score with a d-dimer may be employed in low-risk patients with suspected pulmonary embolism. [17] Imaging tests, such as a ventilation-perfusion scan or chest CTA, are indicated in high-risk individuals.

[85] The differential diagnosis of PE is extensive and includes: Acute coronary syndrome Stable angina Acute pericarditis Congestive heart failure Malignancy Cardiac arrhythmias Pneumonia Pneumonitis Pneumothorax Vasovagal syncope

retrieved_docs.title: Dyspnea on Exertion(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute dyspnea on exertion is most likely caused by: Acute myocardial ischemia Heart failure Cardiac tamponade Pulmonary embolism Pneumothorax Pulmonary infection in the form of bronchitis or pneumonia Upper airway obstruction by aspiration or anaphylaxis Chronic dyspnea is most likely caused by: Asthma Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease Congestive heart failure Interstitial lung disease Myocardial dysfunction Obesity Deconditioning The most common diagnosis underlying dyspnea on exertion is CHF.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Myocardial Infarction -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis can be quite extensive. Factors that increase the likelihood of cardiac disease include exertional pain, diaphoresis, nausea and vomiting, and radiation of pain to the right arm. Other life-threatening illnesses to consider include pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection, pneumothorax, esophageal rupture, and cardiac tamponade. There may be atypical symptoms, especially in women and older patients, so a high suspicion is required. Since symptoms include nausea, vomiting, epigastric pain, and fatigue, it is imperative to consider the cardiac causes of these symptoms and not dismiss these symptoms as gastrointestinal illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Chest Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: When evaluating a patient with symptoms of acute chest syndrome, it is important to consider a broad range of potential causes. The following is a list of differential diagnoses with a description of their symptoms that should be considered: Acute coronary syndrome: conditions such as unstable angina and myocardial infarction, chest pain, shortness of breath, and other cardiac symptoms Pulmonary embolism: sudden-onset chest pain, shortness of breath, and potentially life-threatening symptoms. Pneumothorax: sudden-onset sharp chest pain and difficulty breathing Pneumonia: productive cough, fever, chest pain, and difficulty breathing Pleural effusion: chest pain, shortness of breath, and reduced breath sounds on examination Empyema: chest pain, fever, productive cough, and difficulty breathing Aortic dissection: severe chest pain radiating to the back Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS): difficulty breathing, low oxygen levels, and chest X-ray abnormalities

retrieved_docs.title: Dyspnea on Exertion(Archived) -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Based on possible underlying etiology after the initial evaluation, different specialties can be consulted. As the evaluation and management of dyspnea on exertion is teamwork, the following specialties need to be consulted: Pulmonologist Cardiologist Interventional Radiologist Interventional Cardiologist Thoracic Surgeon

retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary embolism This condition presents with sudden-onset pleuritic chest pain worsened by deep breathing and accompanied by dyspnea, tachypnea, and signs of hemodynamic instability. Risk factors include prolonged immobility and coagulopathies.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism -- History and Physical -- Clinical History
retrieved_docs.content: Less common presentations include arrhythmias (eg, atrial fibrillation), syncope, and hemodynamic collapse. [25] Hemodynamic instability is a rare but essential form of clinical presentation, as it indicates central or extensive PE with severely reduced hemodynamic reserve. Syncope may occur and may be associated with a higher prevalence of hemodynamic instability and right ventricular dysfunction. [26] Recognizing that patients with large PE may sometimes be asymptomatic or have mild symptoms is crucial. PE may often be asymptomatic or discovered incidentally during diagnostic workup for another disease. Therefore, in addition to PE symptoms, clinicians should look for VTE risk factors to determine the clinical probability of PE.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are both classified as vascular abnormalities, reflecting increased pressure within the circulatory system. The broader vascular category also encompasses severe pressure disorders such as أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate a spectrum of vascular pressure elevations that are important for clinicians to recognize, as they can signal acute emergencies or chronic pulmonary vascular disease and guide appropriate monitoring and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is a specific adult respiratory condition that is classified under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2858
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is a chronic condition that commonly presents with elevated mean arterial pressure. It is closely linked to hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), which is considered a severe manifestation of the same underlying disorder. Primary hypertension is also associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), indicating a possible overlap in vascular pathology. Additionally, primary hypertension has a recognized relationship with metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض), reflecting shared risk factors and pathophysiological mechanisms. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians assess cardiovascular risk, monitor for acute hypertensive emergencies, evaluate pulmonary vascular involvement, and address metabolic abnormalities in patients with primary hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both القلق (anxiety) and with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية (abnormal chest X‑ray). Anxiety can amplify the perception of chest discomfort, while an abnormal chest X‑ray may reveal underlying pulmonary or cardiac pathology that also manifests as nonspecific chest pain. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may reflect both a psychological factor (القلق) and a radiographic abnormality (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) is essential for a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with the الأَشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that this particular pattern of signs and symptoms often correlates with findings observed on a chest X‑ray. This relationship guides clinicians to employ chest radiography when the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is present, facilitating the evaluation of underlying thoracic pathology and informing subsequent patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition that reflects an overall increase in arterial pressure. Within the broader vascular category, several specific hypertensive disorders are recognized as subtypes, including أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). Although these entities share the common feature of elevated blood pressure, they differ in their underlying mechanisms, affected vascular territories, and clinical manifestations. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure and its vascular subtypes are interrelated helps clinicians accurately diagnose, monitor, and tailor treatment strategies for patients presenting with various forms of hypertension, thereby improving patient care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) commonly presents with hemodynamic abnormalities such as elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure, both of which are recognized manifestations of the condition. In addition to these direct cardiovascular signs, primary hypertension is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), indicating a link between systemic and pulmonary vascular pressure elevations. Patients with primary hypertension may also exhibit abnormal findings on chest radiography (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية), a clinical association that can aid in the diagnostic evaluation and monitoring of the disease. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations and associations is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of individuals with primary hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2753
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated systolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the same category as several other high‑pressure disorders. Within the vascular domain, أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) represents an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that requires immediate medical intervention. ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) denotes a chronic increase in pressure within the pulmonary arteries that occurs without an identifiable secondary cause. فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) describes a pattern in which only the systolic component of arterial pressure is elevated. Together, Elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, and systolic hypertension are all considered child entities of the broader Vascular classification, highlighting their shared relevance to vascular pathology and the importance of recognizing and managing these distinct forms of high blood pressure in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is defined by the presence of elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure, each of which is a direct manifestation of this condition. Within the vascular domain, these elevated pressure measurements are considered vascular children, reflecting the systemic impact of primary hypertension on the circulatory system. Primary hypertension is also mapped to several related vascular entities: hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي), and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). In addition, primary hypertension is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ) and is clinically linked to abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية). Together, these relationships illustrate how primary hypertension not only elevates key blood pressure parameters but also predisposes patients to acute vascular emergencies, distinct hypertension subtypes, pulmonary vascular disease, and radiographic abnormalities, underscoring the importance of comprehensive vascular assessment and monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2872
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as vascular conditions, and the vascular category also encompasses primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). These entities represent distinct manifestations of abnormal pressure within the circulatory system, illustrating how various forms of hypertension and pulmonary vascular disease fall under the broader umbrella of vascular pathology. Recognizing and managing these vascular pressure abnormalities is essential for comprehensive patient care.
